title: How the Sun Streams a Constant Flow of Charged Particles

lesson.learn: Solar wind is a stream of charged particles that flows outward from the Sun's corona. It is not a strong gust in the everyday sense, but a plasma outflow shaped by the Sun's magnetic field. Fast and slow streams of solar wind help define the heliosphere, the bubble of space influenced by the Sun.
lesson.simpler: The Sun constantly leaks tiny charged particles into space. That stream is called the solar wind.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Solar wind is a plasma flow driven by the Sun's hot corona and magnetic field, and it shapes space weather throughout the Solar System.
lesson.deeperDive: The solar wind is accelerated as the corona expands and magnetic structures open into interplanetary space. Its speed varies with the Sun's magnetic geography: coronal holes tend to produce fast streams, while denser streamer regions produce slower flow. When solar wind reaches Earth, it can compress the magnetosphere, trigger geomagnetic storms, and alter conditions for satellites, radio systems, and auroral activity. That is why solar wind is one of the core topics in space weather forecasting.
lesson.funFact: The solar wind is so thin that it would feel like a vacuum to human senses, even though it can still profoundly affect planets.
references: NASA What Is the Solar Wind?
Marshall Space Flight Center solar wind research
